sentences.text: Well for the time being, everything was normal.
But then everybody got a letter that said service was ended, final.
And we had to leave.
We got a month's wages, I think.
And that was it.
I could, but didn't for a while.
But then my father had friends in the insurance office.
And they actually didn't need me.
So I worked for a very small wages, just so I had something to.
And they were fantastic people.
No, they were not Jewish, but they were very good to me.
After the war, I went back there.
And they helped me.
One gave me a bed.
Everybody chipped in, and they were very good to me.
No, I was at home.
No, but that friend I was talking about, mother's house maid who used to come in, you know, still doing the housework.
And she was good company to me.
And then I found a friend who was a cook, who cooked for that theater, when people were assembled.
And he helped me.
He said, now you come tonight to the restaurant where I am cooking.
And you can peel potatoes.
So you're going to have to be by yourself at night.
So if the Germans come, if they come to pick you up, you are not there.
And you've got a stamp.
You're all right.
It's official.
You work for me, peeling potatoes.
So because we couldn't be in the street after 8:00, we had to stay there, when we were finished with the job.
We just went to sleep.
That's what I did for a while.
And what was life like for you at this time, when you were just living on your own?
Everybody had been taken from you?
Yeah, it was terrible.
Yes, my girlfriend's parents were in that theater.
And I brought them some food.
That's what I did.
Sent parcels to Westerbork.
Because Westerbork was not that bad, the Dutch camp.
To my brother who was-- who was already in Westerbork.
Yes.
Not much.
Can't remember, to tell you the truth.
It's terrible.
I can't remember a lot.
But I know I sent parcels to him.
Until June, 1943 when I was picked up.
And then I went to Westerbork and stayed [INAUDIBLE].
And when I was still, then the following January.
What do you mean, happened?
Registered, of course, put in barracks.
There were-- I was in like the British barracks.
There were Americans.
Like I must tell you, I had the birth certificate, but not a passport.
That's why they kept me.
Because we were useful to them, could be useful to them, as exchange a British for a German.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I'm lost.
That you-- they were going to exhange--
There were British.
There were American.
There were converted people.
There were Gypsies.
They were all kept there, to be sent to a special camp.
Well, we hoped so.
Because my love was still in Amsterdam.
I tried so much to go to the Swiss consulate, and more or less lived there.
And they could have easily given me a passport because they were working to take care for the British.
The British, they couldn't be there, of course.
And after a while, the person I had to deal with, she was for the Germans.
She was against us.
So we never got the passport.
If I would have had a passport, I would not have gone to Bergen-Belsen at all.
I would have gone to good internment camp.
So the person working at the Swiss office was a Nazi sympathizer--
Yeah, yeah.
--who was purposefully not giving people--
Yeah.
And we didn't know that, of course.
Well, I met people there.
You know, become friendly with them.
Not really friends, no, but acquaintances.
He was there, too.
No because of the women were separate of the men, separate barracks.
The food used to come in big [NON -ENGLISH ], they used to call it.
Soup, maybe soup, in the morning coffee.
A piece-- like, quarter of a bread for a couple of days.
Piece of butter, bit of jam, bit of sugar, you know, rations.
But we were allowed to, if you had somebody outside the camp still, you were allowed to receive parcels.
And lots of people did.
But I didn't have anybody there.
So I didn't get parcels.
Not in Westerbork, no.
That's all right in Westerbork, yes.
Yeah, Westerbork was, you know, all right.
Yeah, there was a hospital.
And there was-- yeah, children went to kind of a school.
I took a group of kids, went and played with them, sang with them.
Very young.
Like kindergarten, yeah.
I just applied for it.
The head was a German refugee, of course.
She lived in a beautiful house there.
And she was like, you would say, the director.
No, of that kind-- for the kindergarten.
She was in charge of the children, let me put it that way.
Just do games, and sing with them.
Outside, always outside.
Yeah, they didn't realize.
I used to have lots of children, one day.
And I used to go, a transport in the morning, and there was sometimes nobody left.
Sometimes, two or three kids left.
And I had to start all over again with new kids.
They didn't have much time for that.
Like every week, they went a transport.
So you didn't have longer than a week.
Yes.
Nothing.
Nothing, just the games with them, and sing songs.
And at one time, I was-- you know, in Holland, we have Santa Klaus and Black Peter.
At one time, you know, I was Black Peter at one stage.
But they recognized me from the jumper I had on under the cloak.
No.
Yeah, then I got sick.
I got what they call now hepatitis.
And it was yellow jaundice, they called it.
Well, I was-- I got special rations of sugar and jam because you had to have lots of sweet things.
And then they were talking about, we were going to a very good camp, to Bergen-Belsen.
And we were going in a beautiful train, in a passenger train.
And we got food to go with us.
But I wasn't allowed to go because I was recovering from the yellow jaundice.
And I just could cry because, you know, I missed that.
I missed the good camp.
And they had the train came.
The food went in it.
And well I wasn't to go.
But the train didn't go either.
The train came and left.
And then maybe a week later, it came again.
And then we went in it.
I don't know why they did it.
But what held it up-- I don't know.
Maybe just to pester us, or maybe there was some difficulty.
I don't know.
The Germans, no, not really.
But you could expect anything of them.
No, they had a [GERMAN] there.
Every barrack had somebody who was in charge.
And that was usually one of the German refugees.
Because they lived in the houses.
They were the friends, friends of the Germans.
And they stayed until last.
They helped us to go to the camps, to Germany and Poland.
And they stayed on until last.
Because they were the old ones.
Well, the Germans put them in charge.
None of us would be in charge.
Everything was in the hands of those German Jewish refugees.
So they were actually given--
Preference.
Yes.
Yeah, by the Germans.
They lived not in barracks.
They had their own houses.
And it was built by the Dutchmen, in 1936, to house them.
Oh, OK.
But they were bossy.
And there was a bit of, you know, hate, hatred.
Because we said, look, we did everything for them.
And now they do that to us.
Then, I would just accepted it.
No, no nothing.
No.
They did it on their own, whatever they wanted to do.
Because there were rabbis, you know, and very religious people.
Because there were families together, so what they wanted to do they did amongst them.
But not-- not organized.
Not organized.
Yes.
Yes.
No, not really.
But they all, most of them died.
Yes.
Not from Westerbork.
I know that at one stage, my parents in law came, stayed one day.
And they went on the train.
And an auntie of my husband, fiancee, I shared a bed with her at one stage.
She went in the train.
I saw everybody go.
Terrible.
Really terrible.
No.
To the east, to Poland, to work camp.
But not gas chambers, no, we didn't know.
They didn't tell us anything.
But we knew it was not good.
We'd rather stay in Holland.
Yeah, carrying sand from one end to the other end, and back again.
Just passed the time, I suppose.
If you stayed, you had to do it.
I remember also where, read beans.
They call it reading, you know, sorting them out, beans.
Maybe for factory or something.
I did that for a little while.
That's about all.
I don't think he did anything.
No.
He was a couple of years younger than I was.
But he was not quite-- how would I say-- handicapped.
Not normal, but not bad either.
Maybe these days they would call it ADD, you know?
Attention deficiency.
These days, they would have been able to help him.
But those days-- that's why he died.
He wouldn't stand to camp life, missed the parents.
Yeah, Westerbork, and later in Belsen.
He was very difficult.
Nothing really, just keep an eye on him.
But couldn't do much.
Because he couldn't keep a secret Well, you know, he'd blabber.
I don't know, anything.
It's-- it's very difficult.
He had friends that tried to look after.
The men there helped him shave.
You know, they helped to look after him.
Oh yes, you could trust each other.
No, they were not different nationalities-- nothing-- not in Westerbork.
There was just Dutch.
The Dutch and the Germans.
Yeah.
Of course, there was a bit of a hate for the Germans, even though they were Jewish.
Because they had preference above us.
And we didn't like that.
No.
No.
From June '43 until January '44.
I was 23.
Passenger train, it was all right.
And then, when we arrived, we thought, a good camp.
And straightaway, we saw the SS, with the cross and bone, that regiment.
And I remember the 7 o'clock in the morning, far away, we heard a church bell.
I'll never forget that.
It was so quiet.
And there was a truck standing ready.
And we had a nurse on the train.
And she said, I've got people who had been sick.
And I was sick.
And I want them to go on the truck.
Because the others had to walk for a mile to the camp.
So I got on the truck.
And I had a girlfriend.
She had a little boy.
And she said, you take him.
You take him, So I don't have to carry him.
Little did we know, had that happened in one of the other camps, that whole truck would have gone straight to the gas chambers, that little boy as well.
But there were no gas chambers in Belsen.
So it ended up all right.
I came in the camp.
I went straightaway in a hospital.
They made a hospital available for us.
I was there a few days.
And then I came out.
Could you tell me about the hospital?
Well it wasn't much.
It wasn't ready.
It was just a barrack with a few beds.
But lucky, you know, I wasn't very sick.
I was only recuperating.
Oh, Westerbork was better.
Everything.
And in Belsen they had--
Well, they called it a revere, the hospital.
They had a doctor, a very good doctor, Greek.
Because the Greek were there already, Greek Jews.
And the head of the camp was Greek.
All barracks.
All barracks.
Well, it was out in the middle of a forest.
You know, you could see trees.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was very, very big.
And when you went out of the gate, you know, when people who worked outside the camp had to go out of the gate, and they would walk for miles.
And then there was another camp, but I don't know what was behind.
Political prisoners, Frenchmen, and all different-- Belsen was very, very big.
It had lot of small camps.
None whatsoever.
No.
Just Bergen-Belsen.
Yes.
No, no, no, hold on a minute.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yeah I think so.
I think so anyway.
Yeah, well as I said, my friend gave me the little boy.
I looked after him for a while, while she worked in the kitchen, which was outside the camp.
And I did laundry in the camp for all the old people's home.
That way, I could look after him.
As she could smuggle out some food sometimes.
Very dangerous, but she did it.
I don't know.
On the body, in between two scarves and different ways.
She was very clever.
Bread, bit of sausage.
I can't even remember.
But she did it.
And that all for the child.
I got some.
Terrible.
The toilets, if you can call it toilets, was a long row of holes.
You sat back to back, next to each other.
The latrine, as they called it.
Once in a while, we had to go to the baths, which was outside the camp.
We had a German to come with us.
And we had to undress in front of him in one room, walk to the showers naked.
Yes, just women.
But you know, old women children, anyone.
Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know.
In the beginning, the first few times, you felt terrible.
You walked with a towel like this.
But later on--
That didn't matter.
Didn't matter at all.
Not much.
About similar to Westerbork.
In the morning, you got something that looked like coffee.
But what it was, I don't know.
Some surrogate.
We got a piece of bread, for a few days, your ration of sugar, your ration of butter.
And midday was soup again.
And there was one kind of soup.
And we loved it.
Oh, tonight it's bread-- bread soup.
And it looked brown and thick.
And oh, it was lovely.
It was sweet.
And when everybody had their share, and there was left, you got numbers.
You know, you got a second portion.
The next day, got a second portion.
And we all said, oh, when we're home, we'll come to make the soup.
It was one of our favorites.
God knows what it was.
Yes, but you got used to it.
While lying in bed, we were going to tell all kinds of recipes to each other.
You know, anything.
Food was on your mind all the time, yes.
No.
What do you mean?
I don't know.
We didn't think further than the day we lived in.
We didn't know what was going to happen.
And we didn't care.
And we were numb, kind of.
You know?
You didn't care.
You just lived.
I had a call it-- zombie, kind of zombie.
And then we had to go on roll call in the morning.
And they were counting, and counting, and counting again.
There was one German.
His name was William.
And we used to call him William Tell, you know, because he was counting.
That word, tell, means counting.
And they probably were one short.
And they counted, and counted, and counted.
And we stood there, sometimes in the snow and ice, and the cold.
Eventually it was OK.
Everybody had go to work, who had a job.
Men outside the camp.
As I said, we were numb.
You know?
Anything was good.
Anything was.
It would have been the same every day.
Nothing ever happened, until I got typhoid.
Yes, if you call it laundry.
It was just in the wash house where everybody washed themselves in cold water.
But we tried to keep very clean.
Everybody washing all, every morning.
Yes, cold water and the toilets were in there too.
And they were just overflowing sometimes.
Since we were washing ourselves, we stood in the dirt, you might say.
Yeah, for the old people's home.
I can't remember.
It's terrible, but I can't remember.
I'm sorry.
But I know I did washing.
I don't know how long, but then I went in the shoe commando.
You got a whole heap of old shoes and boots.
And we had gotten a knife.
Everything where it was stitched, undo the stitching.
So you ended up with pieces of leather.
That's what we had to do.
They didn't tell us anything.
And we didn't ask.
And we didn't know.
And we didn't think.
That was the job we had to do.
Heaps, heaps.
Now after the war, we realized, you know, they were from the people who were murdered.
But we didn't think so far ahead at the time.
We also had, at one stage, they had-- they called them bread bags, little bags that probably where they-- if they went out to work, and I had to carry the lunch in.
And apparently, they were damaged with a bomb raid and waterlogged.
And we had to sort them out, the good ones and the bad ones.
And I remember some of the women made bras of them.
Yes.
